source_title: Walk With Weight: Michael Easter On The Evolutionary Case For Rucking, Building Real Resilience & How To Stay Adventure-Ready For Life

paragraphs.speaker: michael
paragraphs.name: Michael Easter
paragraphs.text: Rucking mixes endurance and strength in one. The greatest human rucker is the greatest animal rucker in the entire world. And that's because humans are unique in that we evolved to carry weight for distance. This is the thing that I can finally do that gets me outdoors, that makes me feel good, that has helped me lose weight, that has improved my fitness. Rucking will burn more calories per mile than running. We've kind of missed the boat on this thing that we're really good at and has a ton of benefits.

paragraphs.speaker: rich
paragraphs.name: Rich Roll
paragraphs.text: I want to show you a clip from a podcast I did with your fellow Las Veganite, Alex Honnold. I asked him about rucking and he gave a very interesting response. He said if you don't have to carry weight, don't carry weight. He saw a guy with a 25 pound weight vest walking his dog and thought, 'That's psycho. You're literally just carrying little balls of lead up the mountain and back.'

paragraphs.speaker: michael
paragraphs.name: Michael Easter
paragraphs.text: Alex Honnold is the greatest rock climber of all time in the human world. But if you expand that out to all different animals, he's not good. There's plenty of other primates who would just kick that guy's ass going up. But with rucking, the greatest human rucker is the greatest animal rucker in the entire world. That's because humans are unique in that we evolved to carry weight for distance. So there's no other mammal that can pick up a load and carry a significantly far distance.

paragraphs.speaker: michael
paragraphs.name: Michael Easter
paragraphs.text: Humans evolved to pick up weight and move it for distance. Once we evolved from primates, came out of the trees, there was a climate change happening in the jungle like six million years ago. The primates that could go longer distances were able to find more food and survive. So slowly but surely, natural selection basically favored these primates that could walk on two feet because it was a lot more efficient. And then once we got up onto two feet, all of a sudden we have these free hands and all of a sudden we can start carrying stuff. Humanity really exploded after that because we could take tools into the unknown.

paragraphs.speaker: michael
paragraphs.name: Michael Easter
paragraphs.text: I was out hunting in the Arctic for my book, The Comfort Crisis. We end up hunting this caribou, and we have to pack it back to camp. My pack has 120 pounds of caribou in this thing. And we have to hike it back to our camp across the tundra. It's about five miles. One of the hardest things physically I've ever done. But as I'm doing that, I'm thinking about that idea in the Born to Run papers. Yeah, humans evolved to run so we could hunt. And then, yeah, but then we also have to do this carrying thing.

paragraphs.speaker: rich
paragraphs.name: Rich Roll
paragraphs.text: From a health perspective, give me the reasons why rucking is something you're so bullish on compared to hiking, jogging, or running.

paragraphs.speaker: michael
paragraphs.name: Michael Easter
paragraphs.text: When it comes to why not just run, I'll make two key points. The first would be injuries. There's one famous study that found that 79% of runners get injured in a year. That's really high. But when you look at walking with a weighted pack or a weight vest, injury rate is way lower. The second point is that rucking mixes endurance and strength in one. You're getting endurance because you're covering ground. But you're also getting a strength effect because you have weight on your body.

paragraphs.speaker: michael
paragraphs.name: Michael Easter
paragraphs.text: When you're out in an environment that you have to navigate effectively, you're taxing your spatial navigation. That navigation is really important for brain health. There was a study conducted in the UK that looked at about 400 different jobs and their risk of Alzheimer's and dementia. The jobs with the lowest rates were cab drivers and ambulance drivers. I think the reason for that is that these people are constantly having to navigate all these new environments.

paragraphs.speaker: michael
paragraphs.name: Michael Easter
paragraphs.text: Rucking, I would say, is walking with weight in a backpack or a weight vest. I have started to use more the term walking with weight because the term rucking came out of the military. When I started to use the term around my mom or my wife, they Google rucking and they get these soldiers with these 150-pound backpacks. So I've started to shift into walking with weight to make it a little more approachable.

paragraphs.speaker: michael
paragraphs.name: Michael Easter
paragraphs.text: If you had to choose one, I would steer people into a backpack. With a weight vest loaded in your front and your back, once you get tired, your posture starts to just go to absolute shit.
